old_value:
{"id":"project-LICHEN-gom9","title":"GPSR fallback uses expired gradient entries for coordinates","description":"File: python/src/lichen/routing/router.py:480-484

When LOADng is not configured, GPSR fallback looks up gradient entries WITHOUT the 'now' parameter:

```python
dst_entry = self.gradient_table.lookup(dst)  # may be expired but has coords
if dst_entry is not None and dst_entry.coords is not None:
    next_hop = self.gpsr_forward(dst_entry.coords)
```

The comment acknowledges this returns potentially expired entries.

WHY IT'S A BUG: Using coordinates from an expired entry means routing based on stale location data. In a mobile mesh, a node could have moved significantly since the entry expired. GPSR would forward toward the node's old position, causing packets to be routed to the wrong location.

IMPACT: Misdirected packets in mobile deployments when GPSR fallback is used. Security concern: a compromised node could advertise false coordinates, then let the entry expire while GPSR continues routing based on those coordinates.

FIX: Either reject expired coords entirely, or add a separate 'coord_expires' field that allows a grace period for location data distinct from route freshness.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"me@mark.atwood.name","created_at":"2026-07-12T06:23:11.595062Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","updated_at":"2026-07-12T06:23:11.595099Z"}

{"id":"project-LICHEN-gom9","title":"GPSR fallback uses expired gradient entries for coordinates","description":"File: python/src/lichen/routing/router.py:480-484

When LOADng is not configured, GPSR fallback looks up gradient entries WITHOUT the 'now' parameter:

```python
dst_entry = self.gradient_table.lookup(dst)  # may be expired but has coords
if dst_entry is not None and dst_entry.coords is not None:
    next_hop = self.gpsr_forward(dst_entry.coords)
```

The comment acknowledges this returns potentially expired entries.

WHY IT'S A BUG: Using coordinates from an expired entry means routing based on stale location data. In a mobile mesh, a node could have moved significantly since the entry expired. GPSR would forward toward the node's old position, causing packets to be routed to the wrong location.

IMPACT: Misdirected packets in mobile deployments when GPSR fallback is used. Security concern: a compromised node could advertise false coordinates, then let the entry expire while GPSR continues routing based on those coordinates.

FIX: Either reject expired coords entirely, or add a separate 'coord_expires' field that allows a grace period for location data distinct from route freshness.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"AI Agent","owner":"me@mark.atwood.name","created_at":"2026-07-12T06:23:11.595062Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","updated_at":"2026-07-22T20:20:42.530568515Z","started_at":"2026-07-22T20:20:42.530568515Z","lease_expires_at":"2026-07-22T20:25:42.530568515Z","heartbeat_at":"2026-07-22T20:20:42.530568515Z"}
new_value: {"notes":"Commit: 36c5e7ff5\nUpdated GPSR fallback in _route_mesh_local to explicitly pass now_ms to gradient_table.lookup (rejects expired coords). Enhanced comment with security note. Matches lookup behavior used for primary routing path."}
GPSR fallback now passes now_ms to gradient_table.lookup() (rejects expired entries with stale coords). Updated comments. Matches primary routing path behavior and RFC intent for fresh location data.
